column1: # TABLE 1: SPEED SWEEP - generation tok/s by context size (aiperf synthetic sweep
column5: 10 requests/size). tps = E2E output token throughput per user (includes TTFT). base cells = original recording (raw base artifacts removed); dflash + ngram cells re-extracted from artifacts/ 2026-07. NOTE: the dflash 36864 point was re-run 2026-07 and measures much slower than the original recording (E2E 273.04
column6: ITL 3.26 - worth re-running to confirm). ngram accept on synthetic prompts is only 9-14% (random tokens = nothing to copy).

column4: one server on the GPU at a time). gen_tps = per-user decode throughput during the run.

column12: clean paired run, n_max=10; same 100 problems as base, 3.75x gen speed
full set, n_max=15; same 500 problems, 3.49x gen speed / 3.06x wall

# TABLE 3: LEADERBOARD RUNS (10 short requests; n_max = --spec-draft-n-max; baseline = qwen3.6-27B @ 70.34 tok/s). ngram row: short code-ish prompt is the ngram best case (81% accept).
# TABLE 4: CODING ACCURACY - official LiveCodeBench pass@1 (release_v5
column5: code executed locally by the harness). Small sample - the 1-problem gap is noise
column6: the takeaway is parity. CAVEAT: both runs used lcb_runner's default --max_tokens 2000
column7: which truncated 47/58 (base) and 46/58 (dflash) generations mid-reasoning before any code block -> auto-fail; every generation that fit passed (11/11 and 12/12). The A/B parity is fair (identical truncation) but absolute pass@1 is understated - re-run with --max_tokens 16000 for real capability numbers.

column3: seed 42). Single-turn real prompts: ngram adds nothing over dflash here (no repeated context to copy). The 30-request replay artifacts were deleted; its row comes from the results.md log paste.

column2: maint 10-18 = working on the existing code)
column4: 2026-07-12. decode/maint/e2e in tok/s; the baseline SLOWS as context grows (early->late) while ngram stacks ACCELERATE (more context = more to copy).
